The prosecutor said he would not have prosecuted the prisoner save that the constables said he was in bad company. crime: stealing 5 linen frocks etc.
grounds_clemency: age (49 years) 'in ill heath and infirm'.
additional_info: 4. Mary Ayres, for stealing 5 linen frocks etc, 7 years transportation. age (49 years) 'in ill heath and infirm'. Report of Brook Watson, Mayor of London on the 'Return of Women Convicts in Newgate, where ages or infirmities will not admit of their being Transported' Signed by F. Ramsden, surgeon. John Kirby, Newgate Keeper writes, 'There are none of the 23 Women here mentioned that are of bad behaviour, and not one of them, except Jane Wiggins (who has been convicted once before) were ever before in Custody.' as the prisoners cannot 'bear a Voyage to Botany Bay' and 'their Crimes of a nature not deserving further Punishment in Newgate..., could they be sent to the Penitentiary House at Clarkenwell, they would there be in a much less Afflicting situation'. If this is not possible then a free pardon to be granted and the women sent to their respective parishes. The report is annotated 'let all the Women named in the Enclosed List be pardoned to be passed to their several parishes, except Mary Wilson and Jane Wiggins alias Young who are to be pardoned on Condition of being confined for the remainder of the term of their sentences in the House of Correction Cold Bath fields.' Folios 192-195.

crime: vagrancy and offences against the Lottery Act
initial_sentence: 3 months further imprisonment in the Bridewell, after a previous confinement.
grounds_clemency: previous good character, Hughes could not have written any thing down, as he could not read or write, [he signed the petition with a cross mark], both prisoners were innocent and the prosecutors had sworn falsely against them.
additional_info: Evidences supplied by Gabriel Franks and Moses Solomons, jews. They swore in court that Hughes had set down an illegal insurance and that Marshal had taken money for it. The prisoners state that the prosecutors had sworn falsely against them. The prisoners explain that just before the prisoners were arrested a man had come into the offices saying he had a warrant for illegal practices and enquired if the masters of the business were on the premises. When he was told they were not, the man offered to prevent the warrants execution in return for payment. This was refused and the man said the parties would swear to illegal practices anyway. This appears to have happened and the Franks and Solomons swore to the evidences given against the prisoners for illegal practices. The prisoners have knowledge that the man's name was Phillips and he is connected with the Franks and Solomons, and Phillips 'was the Companion of the late notorious Convict Lawrence Jones'. There is an affidavit from Abraham Nathan and his wife Ann Nathan, of St Botolph, Aldgate, to the effect that Franks had told them of 'a Business by which some money might be got'. A man called Levy had obtained 30 guineas from a Lottery Office Keeper by examining a lottery number and then going back and saying an illegal insurance had been made on the examined number and saying a warrant would be obtained illegal practices but stopped the warrant in exchange for money. Franks said to Nathan that this scenario 'would come better from a Woman,' and asked Nathan 'to let his wife go with said Franks', to which Nathan and his wife agreed. Ann Nathan was given a piece of paper with a high number on it so that the clerk would have to check to see if it was drawn (it was the custom of the clerks to remember the first hundred or so and they would not have to leave to check these. After Franks left the woman told her husband that they 'would not have anything to do with the business'. Franks called again and told the husband and wife that he had sent Moses and Solomons, instead of Ann Nathan, and the parties had refused to give money so the parties would 'go down' [be imprisoned].

crime: stealing 20 guineas and 4 doubloons (the doubloons, value 14:8:0)
grounds_clemency: spent a long time in prison awaiting transportation because of difficulties in transporting prisoners.
additional_info: Brown was an army sergeant just returned from Gibraltar. The prosecutor was tricked out of the money by an 'Artful (tho' common) device' of being approached by a stranger, the 2 of them then finding or discovering a supposed valuable item (I this case a paste ring appearing as a real diamond - with a supposed receipt) and being led to part with money for the goods. The jury did not find him guilty of stealing from a dwelling house and so made the conviction non-capital. The case was reserved for the 'opinion of the Judges' to decide if it was a felony or fraud; they ruled it was a felony. The judge considered the prisoner to be 'an artful + practised swindler'.

crime: breaking and entering the dwelling house
grounds_clemency: none given.
additional_info: 1. James Connell, convicted at the Old Bailey[?] for breaking and entering the dwelling house of the prosecutor. Prisoner was respited for 3 days for an investigation to be carried out for a 'supposed variance' between the indictment and evidence; the indictment was for breaking and entering the house as 'no person being therein' was accepted as 'correct'. At the same time a woman was present in the 'garrett of the house'. As the whole house was let out in separate tenements 'in point of Law' each apartment was the dwelling house of the occupier. Thus the evidence of no person being left in the prosecutor's apartment, which was his dwelling house, supported the indictment. none given. death. no mercy, unless the judge receives orders to the contrary (, the judge is to issue a warrant for Connell to be executed the following Thursday with 17 other 'unfortunate' prisoners).

After court appearances at Bristol Assizes[?] in April 1785 and September 1786 they were discharged since no prosecutor appeared. They were subsequently sent to London as their place of settlement. On 19 December 1786 Randall was tried and acquitted at the Old Bailey of being at large without lawful cause but detained to be transported depending on a pardon. Potten has absconded. free pardon for both women.]

additional_info: Ives has since been executed. He later gave himself up on 13 June 1789 and was remanded in prison. crime: piracy at the Sessions House in the Old Bailey on 18 November 1816
grounds_clemency: the prisoner's were strongly recommended to mercy by the prosecutor and the jury at the time of the trial, there were no members of the crew in England at the time of the trial, the incident happened through the misconduct of the captain, council did not give all the facts at the trial, witness Boyd called it a mutiny when this was not the case; a 'reward' would be given in salvage if any prisoners could be convicted, respectable persons have signed the petition and Hastings was wounded at the Battle of Trafalgar.
additional_info: The Roebuck, property of John George Nicholls was bound for England from Sierra Leone on the coast of Africa and had a cargo of gold dust and elephant's teeth when the crew staged a mutiny. There is a covering letter from J D Ullmer to: sworn statements by James Ullmer, solicitor to the prisoner's; John Came and James Davis; a letter [mostly illegible] from Captain Galway; a certificate of wounds (received by Hastings on board the Roebuck) from George Imlay, surgeon; a certificate of good behaviour for William Hasten [William Hastings] from George Anthony, late master of the Carlebury; a certificate of 'mortification in his feet' whilst on board the Penelopy for Bruce from Charles Hepturn, surgeon; a copy of the evidence taken at the trial with observations by Ullmer; a detailed statement of the case 'as delivered by the prisoner's themselves'; a letter from Hastings to Sir Isarel Pelow [Israel Pellow], where Hastings [Hasten] states he was on board HMS Conqueror a at the Battle of Trafalgar.

additional_info: Evidences supplied by Alphonso Maclean and John Barron. There was a long delay before the prosecutor took action against the prisoner as the prisoner's wife had persuaded the prosecutor not to persue the case [reason not given]. The prisoner is thought to be part of a gang, another member of the gang concerned with the crime, a Mr Wallis, has been convicted for highway robbery. There is a covering letter from Mr James of Gray's Inn Place and a letter from Thomas Swinburne, 'a relative' confirming that the prisoner has future employment if released.

crime: the theft of a pair of spectacles
grounds_clemency: previous good character, first offence, had 2 or 3 fatherless children, her former employer was so sure of her innocence he would give any security for her, her prosecutors are 'Common informers', and John Kirby, gaoler of Newgate, had a favourable opinion of her.
additional_info: It is claimed by the former employer that the prisoner found the spectacles, which had previously been picked from the pocket of a lady during the Lord Mayor's Show. There is a letter from Richard Borough asking for an enquiry into the case of the prisoner, and a further letter asking for details of the prisoners state. There is a letter from John King to William Batthone giving details that the evidence against the prisoner was supplied by two 'notorious Jews' who should not have been permitted to give evidence and had both been previously tried at the Old Bailey. There is a covering letter from William Batthone to a note from Mr Kirby [wanting]

grounds_clemency: age (43 years), 'infirm +c'.
additional_info: 23. Mary Ash, for stealing a silver watch, convicted in 1795, transportation for life. age (43 years), 'infirm +c'. Report of Brook Watson, Mayor of London on the 'Return of Women Convicts in Newgate, where ages or infirmities will not admit of their being Transported' Signed by F. Ramsden, surgeon. John Kirby, Newgate Keeper writes, 'There are none of the 23 Women here mentioned that are of bad behaviour, and not one of them, except Jane Wiggins (who has been convicted once before) were ever before in Custody.' as the prisoners cannot 'bear a Voyage to Botany Bay' and 'their Crimes of a nature not deserving further Punishment in Newgate..., could they be sent to the Penitentiary House at Clarkenwell, they would there be in a much less Afflicting situation'. If this is not possible then a free pardon to be granted and the women sent to their respective parishes. The report is annotated 'let all the Women named in the Enclosed List be pardoned to be passed to their several parishes, except Mary Wilson and Jane Wiggins alias Young who are to be pardoned on Condition of being confined for the remainder of the term of their sentences in the House of Correction Cold Bath fields.' Folios 192-195.

grounds_clemency: nine people were tried of similar charges and all had different punishments; Ryley Perry has a sick wife to support and 2 infant children
additional_info: William Campion, Thomas Ryley Perry and John Clark, convicted at the Old Bailey in June and July 1824 for publishing blasphemous libel, especially the 'Theological works of Thomas Paine' and Palmers 'Principles of nature'. Also included is a letter from William Campion in which he states that William Haley who received the same sentence was set free because he changed his opinions and Campion accuses him of hypocrisy. Campion also states that it was made clear in his trial that the prosecution was not 'against individuals for holding such opinions, but for publishing them' and as Haley had also published the material he should not have been set free. nine people were tried of similar charges and all had different punishments; Ryley Perry has a sick wife to support and 2 infant children None recorded for Campion or Clark. Three years imprisonment in Newgate for Ryley Perry. None given for Campion or Clerk. No mercy for Thomas Ryley Perry. Folios 134-145.

John Kirby, Newgate Keeper writes, 'There are none of the 23 Women here mentioned that are of bad behaviour, and not one of them, except Jane Wiggins (who has been convicted once before) were ever before in Custody.' as the prisoners cannot 'bear a Voyage to Botany Bay' and 'their Crimes of a nature not deserving further Punishment in Newgate..., could they be sent to the Penitentiary House at Clarkenwell, they would there be in a much less Afflicting situation'. The report is annotated 'let all the Women named in the Enclosed List be pardoned to be passed to their several parishes, except Mary Wilson and Jane Wiggins alias Young who are to be pardoned on Condition of being confined for the remainder of the term of their sentences in the House of Correction Cold Bath fields.' initial_sentence: 7 years transportation to America (changed to Africa by the Order of the King in Council).

additional_info: 6. Edward Jones and John Hammond, both indicted on 15 September 1784, Jones for stealing and (not for burglary) and Hammond for receiving a number of unfinished watches and watch movements of considerable value, property of William Tash. According to the evidence the prosecutor's workshop had been broken into during the night. none given. Jones for 7 years transportation to America and Hammond for 14 years transportation to America. no mercy 7. Jones Jackson, indicted on 7 July 1784, for committing burglary in the house of John Morris and stealing a shift and some other articles of small value. The prisoner was acquitted of burglary, for the time at which the offence was committed altered the nature of the offence. The crime was committed 'a little before day light in the evening was quite gone' and was convicted for stealing the items. the prisoner has suffered much in prison, the circumstances for mitigation attested by the prosecutor and others (names and documents not included) have been taken into consideration, the prisoner's extreme state of poverty and distress impelled him to commit this offence. 7 years transportation to America (changed to Africa by the Order of the King in Council). to transport himself for the term of sentence. Although the judge speaks of recommending Jackson for a free pardon, he claims that as the prisoner has no means by which he can financially support himself or his family he may commit further offences.

Evidences supplied by John Yerworth, foreman to Messrs. Salter and Coopers: Richard Jones, bookkeeper on board the Young John: John Gillman, Thames Police Officer: John Fox, Thames Police Constable: Thomas Taylor, lighterman and Henry Salter, lighterman. Hall is of previous good character and holds a position of trust, he has a wife and 5 infant children to support and a previous employer is willing to employ him again; Gray supports a wife and 2 children as well as his blind mother, has been recommended to Lady Hawkesbury by the Countess of Cardigan and he is willing to enter the navy (has been offered a place by George Anthony). death, commuted to transportation by 'the kind intersession of her ladyship the Countess of Cardigan'. no further mercy. crime: the theft of silver plate (by way of pledging it) property of William Gray, jeweller, of New Bond Street, London, on 17 November 1800
grounds_clemency: youth (23 years), the prisoner has a wife and child, the prisoner's family will be destitute without the prisoner's assistance, prisoners wife had gone into premature labour due to anxiety and is unable to support herself and the prisoner wishes to transport himself.
additional_info: The prisoner sent the 'duplicates' [pawn brokers tickets] back to Grey anonymously. The prosecutor published a message in a newspaper the following day saying if the prisoner came to see him 'he shall meet with every thing to His Satisfaction.' The prosecutor accepted the prisoner's explanation for the stealing of the goods and offered to assist the prisoner in going abroad. The prisoner was arrested for the crime sometime later. There are five letters from Kate Smith, prisoner's wife, to Lady Pelham; Lord Pelham [3 letters] and Sir George Shee, there are mentions of prisoner Cooper, who received only 6 months imprisonment for stealing from Messrs Jeffery & Gilbert, goldsmiths; and representations on behalf of the prisoner from Mr Lamb, MP for Rye, Sussex. There are three covering letters to recommendations, on behalf of the prisoner; 1 of which mentions a former employer, Louis Recordon, watchmaker to the Prince of Wales (his signature is on the petition). There are letters of recommendation from Colonel Crewe (stating the prisoner is a suitable person to serve his majesty and that the prisoner would have remained in the regiment had it not been 'disemboydied;'). There are also references to Thomas John Southcote, Baronet; and William Grey, prosecutor. There is a letter from J. Davis Lamb to Kate Smith wishing her luck with her mothering and offering assistance to her husband.

Report of Brook Watson, Mayor of London on the 'Return of Women Convicts in Newgate, where ages or infirmities will not admit of their being Transported' Signed by F. Ramsden, surgeon. John Kirby, Newgate Keeper writes, 'There are none of the 23 Women here mentioned that are of bad behaviour, and not one of them, except Jane Wiggins (who has been convicted once before) were ever before in Custody.' as the prisoners cannot 'bear a Voyage to Botany Bay' and 'their Crimes of a nature not deserving further Punishment in Newgate..., could they be sent to the Penitentiary House at Clarkenwell, they would there be in a much less Afflicting situation'. If this is not possible then a free pardon to be granted and the women sent to their respective parishes. The report is annotated 'let all the Women named in the Enclosed List be pardoned to be passed to their several parishes, except Mary Wilson and Jane Wiggins alias Young who are to be pardoned on Condition of being confined for the remainder of the term of their sentences in the House of Correction Cold Bath fields.' additional_info: He entered the shop on the pretence of 'buying some small article' and hid the stolen goods under his coat. The judge stated that the crime was not caused by immediate want as the prisoner had money when he was captured. The jury reduced the value of the stolen goods to 10d. The judge considers that 'notwithstanding the appearance of repentance, which he [the prisoner] has ability enough to assume, he seems to me, a very dangerous man to be let upon the public'. The judge also states that the prisoner had lived, as clerk, with the late Mr Cowper, the Clerk of the Rules in the Court of King's Bench. Cowper's chambers had at some time been broken into and the judge suspected the prisoner as taking a leading role in that crime.

crime: bigamy, in unlawfully marrying at Whitechapel Church in Middlesex, Elizabeth Provost, spinster, (now called Elizabeth Dubordieux) on 25 April 1801 having already a wife alive, Esther Foster, married at 'Milten next Gravesend' [Milton], Kent, on 13 Septemb
grounds_clemency: the prisoner was born in Bengal to French parents and was sent to Europe for his education at a young age, he was tricked into marriage by a prostitute when only 18, he was unaware of the laws of this country, the prisoner believes the prosecution was carried out by Abraham Provost to obtain money and property from the prisoner (it was suggested that the prisoner give all his household furniture and 50 per annum for the life of Elizabeth which was agreed but the prosecutor changed his mind).
additional_info: The prisoner was prosecuted by Abraham Provost, against the wishes of Elizabeth. Evidences supplied by Edmund Foster, brother of Esther Foster; Elizabeth Provost, Stephen Payne, constable; and Abraham Provost, father of Elizabeth, journeyman cooper. There is a letter from John Kirby, principal gaoler of Newgate who lists people who will give Esther Foster a bad character. Abraham Provost stated that his daughter 'had no great fortune.' The prisoner has declared to one of the witnesses that he had a third wife. There is an affidavit from Sarah Hutchinson (then Sarah Thurslow) stating that in 1795 Esther Forster had lied that she was pregnant by Charles Dubordieux and that he married Forster to save her reputation. There is an affidavit from Sarah Duffin stating that Forster was a common prostitute and lived with a man called Brown. There is a statement of Charles Dubordieux (and 1 copy) proving the prostitution of his wife Esther Foster. The prisoner arrived in England aboard the Marquis of Landsdowne, and was 'ensnared by the machinations of a woman, well acquainted with the wiles of the world, and - possessing no small share of Beauty...' Shortly after the wedding the prisoner contracted 'a disease.' The physician cured him of the same disease twice. The prisoner separated from his wife and thought that it was probable that she was dead from disease due to her living as a prostitute.
doc_description: Report of John William Rose, Recorder of London, on  petitions  on behalf of Charles Dubordieux, clerk, native of Calcutta, convicted at the Old Bailey in September for bigamy, in unlawfully marrying at Whitechapel Church in Middlesex, Elizabeth Provost, spinster, (now called Elizabeth Dubordieux) on 25 April 1801 having already a wife alive, Esther Foster, married at 'Milten next Gravesend' [Milton], Kent, on 13 September 1795

John Kirby, Newgate Keeper writes, 'There are none of the 23 Women here mentioned that are of bad behaviour, and not one of them, except Jane Wiggins (who has been convicted once before) were ever before in Custody.' as the prisoners cannot 'bear a Voyage to Botany Bay' and 'their Crimes of a nature not deserving further Punishment in Newgate..., could they be sent to the Penitentiary House at Clarkenwell, they would there be in a much less Afflicting situation'. If this is not possible then a free pardon to be granted and the women sent to their respective parishes. The report is annotated 'let all the Women named in the Enclosed List be pardoned to be passed to their several parishes, except Mary Wilson and Jane Wiggins alias Young who are to be pardoned on Condition of being confined for the remainder of the term of their sentences in the House of Correction Cold Bath fields.' Folios 192-195.

crime: stealing 13 iron bars, value 7/-, property of Richard Crawshaw, William Crawshaw and William Thompson, ironmongers, on 27 January 1793.

additional_info: Evidences supplied by John Harrison, patrolman of Castle Baynard; John Chambers, John Billing, clerk to Messrs Crawshaw, Son and Thompson, ironmongers; Robert Mahanam and others. Randall claimed he was set up as the iron was put as a trap by the Clerk of the Wharf. The Clerk had frequently threatened revenge on the prisoner, as he had been a witness against 2 smugglers at Deptford. As a result of giving evidence his house was set on fire, an enquiry was held and he was awarded 20 in part compensation for the loss. The subpoena to appear to give evidence against Alexandra Buchannan (1 of the smugglers) on behalf of HM. Customs is attached to the petition. There is also an note signed by Admiral Gardiner, concerning Randall's good behaviour on the Lion and attesting to his ability to 'steer & heave the Lead'

crime: stealing a pocket book.
grounds_clemency: age (51 years) 'subject to fits and infirm'.
additional_info: 7. Hannah Findall, for stealing a pocket book, convicted May 1793, 7 years transportation. age (51 years) 'subject to fits and infirm'. Report of Brook Watson, Mayor of London on the 'Return of Women Convicts in Newgate, where ages or infirmities will not admit of their being Transported' Signed by F. Ramsden, surgeon. John Kirby, Newgate Keeper writes, 'There are none of the 23 Women here mentioned that are of bad behaviour, and not one of them, except Jane Wiggins (who has been convicted once before) were ever before in Custody.' as the prisoners cannot 'bear a Voyage to Botany Bay' and 'their Crimes of a nature not deserving further Punishment in Newgate..., could they be sent to the Penitentiary House at Clarkenwell, they would there be in a much less Afflicting situation'. If this is not possible then a free pardon to be granted and the women sent to their respective parishes. The report is annotated 'let all the Women named in the Enclosed List be pardoned to be passed to their several parishes, except Mary Wilson and Jane Wiggins alias Young who are to be pardoned on Condition of being confined for the remainder of the term of their sentences in the House of Correction Cold Bath fields.' Folios 192-195.

additional_info: convicted at the 'last' Admiralty Sessions of the Old Bailey for the theft of dollars belonging to Messrs Holford, Gonne and others from the vessel Maria while on the high seas [piracy]. There is a covering letter from H C Litchfield of Lincolns Inn to the statement of a case which has been laid before the attorney general and the Solicitor general; a statement of the case with the opinions of Vicary Gibbs and Thomas Plumer; 2 copies of the pardon on condition of transportation for life; a letter from and W M Russell stating he has re-examined Peter Johnson, the chief witness; 3 copies of a letter from Thomas Skelton, clerk of arraigns asking whether the Admiralty have the jurisdiction to make an order for transportation; a memo and a letter from Mr Russell asking whether the prisoner is to be transported [as a felon] or whether the prisoner will self-transport. The prisoner's petition contains a detailed account of the crime.

additional_info: 14. Ann Gibbons, for stealing a blanket etc, convicted February 1795, 7 years transportation. age (43 years), 'infirm'. Report of Brook Watson, Mayor of London on the 'Return of Women Convicts in Newgate, where ages or infirmities will not admit of their being Transported' Signed by F. Ramsden, surgeon. John Kirby, Newgate Keeper writes, 'There are none of the 23 Women here mentioned that are of bad behaviour, and not one of them, except Jane Wiggins (who has been convicted once before) were ever before in Custody.' as the prisoners cannot 'bear a Voyage to Botany Bay' and 'their Crimes of a nature not deserving further Punishment in Newgate..., could they be sent to the Penitentiary House at Clarkenwell, they would there be in a much less Afflicting situation'. If this is not possible then a free pardon to be granted and the women sent to their respective parishes. The report is annotated 'let all the Women named in the Enclosed List be pardoned to be passed to their several parishes, except Mary Wilson and Jane Wiggins alias Young who are to be pardoned on Condition of being confined for the remainder of the term of their sentences in the House of Correction Cold Bath fields.' Folios 192-195.

crime: an assault in Aldgate Street on David Richards with intent to steal from him, on 6 November 'last'
grounds_clemency: potential mistaken identity.
additional_info: Thomas Bates and George Hooker convicted at the Old Bailey on 14 December 'last', for an assault in Aldgate Street on David Richards with intent to steal from him, on 6 November 'last'. Bardon, has been informed that 2 other prisoners Edward Fox and William Olliver had confessed to the crime before they had been executed. The judge states that in general such claims by prisoners 'do not in general deserve much credit', but as the different statements were made at different times, and in different places, it was thought this may indicate a genuine case of mistaken identity, even though the prisoners were 'bad men'. potential mistaken identity. 7 years transportation to Africa. no direct recommendation - free pardon if innocent or 7 years transportation (with a more favourable climate than Africa) if doubts regarding the evidence still exist.

additional_info: 21. Elizabeth Harrell, for petit larceny, convicted January 1797, 7 years transportation. age (47 years) 'in very ill health'. John Kirby, Newgate Keeper writes, 'There are none of the 23 Women here mentioned that are of bad behaviour, and not one of them, except Jane Wiggins (who has been convicted once before) were ever before in Custody.' Recommendation: as the prisoners cannot 'bear a Voyage to Botany Bay' and 'their Crimes of a nature not deserving further Punishment in Newgate..., could they be sent to the Penitentiary House at Clarkenwell, they would there be in a much less Afflicting situation'. If this is not possible then a free pardon to be granted and the women sent to their respective parishes. The report is annotated 'let all the Women named in the Enclosed List be pardoned to be passed to their several parishes, except Mary Wilson and Jane Wiggins alias Young who are to be pardoned on Condition of being confined for the remainder of the term of their sentences in the House of Correction Cold Bath fields.'

crime: a violent assault on and obstructing John Twentyman, Supervisor of the Excise, and William Odger, Officer of the Customs, and others (names given) on 5 May 1799
initial_sentence: death, recommended to mercy by jury because he used no violence.
grounds_clemency: too much liquor (drunk), inflammatory talk, took no part in smuggling, he worked as a pilot in the English channel, his brother and father have defended coast against contraband gangs, was penitent, had a wife and 3 children, good conduct in Newgate, is the superintendent of prisoners, good character from gaol keeper, is willing to serve in HM. Forces, Captain Bircher of Vlieter will take him, Lords of the Admiralty have no objection to him joining HM. Navy and the prisoner has previously served on the Andromeda under Captain Salisbury.
additional_info: There is a copy of the report of the Solicitor to the Board. The customs officials had found brandy and 'geneva' (Hollands Geneva - called Gin when distilled in England) buried in trenches in a field occupied by Henry Roberts, a smuggler, of St Kevern in Cornwall. The smugglers resisted the seizing of the buried goods, they were thought to be part of crews belonging to Alexander and Providence two smuggling luggers which were captured by Active and Dolphin two Revenue cutters. The smugglers made off with the liquor and 'set them down openly in the Town and drank out of them..... the Officers not being strong enough to oppose such a Number of men armed, left them in Possession of the Goods.' There is a covering letter to the petition from R. J. Wheeler to Lord Arden. There is a letter from Lord Arden of the Admiralty Office.

label: FAVOURABLE

crime: receiving) a number of small articles of 'saddlers' goods
grounds_clemency: none given.
additional_info: 2. John Wintersgale, convicted for stealing (and his father for receiving) a number of small articles of 'saddlers' goods, value 38/-, property of his employer Mr Gibson, saddler. The items being found in the fathers' house and shop some of which were concealed. The offence was greatly aggravated by: a) the son being an employee of the prosecutor. b) The receiver and the thief standing in the relation of 'father and son'. The jury confined their verdict to 2 small articles more obvious and proven than the rest and reduced the value to 10 pence, and being bound by a 'point of law' the jury acquitted the father 'there being no accessory in petty larceny'. none given. not given. no mercy. Folios 64-65. See also HO 47/3/18, folios 59-60 and HO 47/3/32, folios 104-105 for Wintersgale.

crime: footpad robbery in September 1784[?]
grounds_clemency: youth (17 years), was very poor and miserable, was innocent of the crime (testified by Rellions and Collop), the prosecutor 6 weeks after the incident claimed to be able to identify the assailant after seeing him for an instance on a moonlit night, the prosecutor was tempted by the sharing of a 40 reward for each prisoner he was to successfully convict, further in the prosecutor's evidence he spoke of 'two men', Rellions and Collop were 'stout lads' of about 20 years old whilst Abel was 17 years old very slight and low of stature.
additional_info: The prosecutor was a labourer from gravel lane the known haunt of the 'lewd and worst' of the people, was robbed at 10 o'clock in a field on a moonlit night. The prosecutor was stunned on the first blow he received from Rellions. Evidences supplied by William Rellions and William Collop (the latter was convicted for another robbery and executed in early November 1784) whose declaration to prove Abel innocent was \"uniform consistent and persistent\\\"\

crime: receiving 3 gallons of oil of turpentine knowing it to have been stolen, value 18/-, property of John Shuttleworth
grounds_clemency: the prisoner has a wife and child to support, did not know the turpentine was stolen, the signatories to the petition are respectable and the prisoner is deaf.  it was customary for such sales of paint to be made, the prosecutor is satisfied with the punishment already served and the prosecutor thinks the prisoner may have been unaware that the goods were stolen.
additional_info: Evidences supplied by John Shuttleworth, manufacturer of oil of turpentine in Sun Tavern Fields; Rupert Clark, magistrate; William Sevier, painter and glazier; Ann Runsley, mother in law to the prisoner; Ann Cullen, landlady to Sevier; Robert Brown, officer of Shadwell police office; George Hillery, John Crutchfield and Mr Banks. There are covering letters to the petitions from Thomas Warburton, former master of the prisoner; Anton Chaplin and S. Bernard. There is a letter from John Kirby of Newgate prison, listing respectable friends of Carutthers (mostly from Hoxton) and also listing John Hillier's friends (mostly from Wapping and Shadwell). The letter also states that the prison has 700 inmates, 5 in the infirmary and requests 'an Order for the removal of 80, as the expense will be little more than taking 60'. There is a note of the prices of Oil of Turpentine 'to Gallop and Co' sent by G. Tucker. There is a statement of good character from John Kirby, Keeper of Newgate, dated 7 July 1804 and a covering letter to a petition from the minister and churchwardens of St. Leonard's, Shoreditch.

grounds_clemency: the prisoner had been escaping from 4 years confinement by the French when he was captures by the English, the prisoner had purchased an 'American Protection' to obtain release, he was placed by the French into American custody, put aboard an American ship that was captured by the Nimrod cutter (English), the prisoner did not know of England's war with America, the jury were not made aware of the circumstances at the time of the trial, the prisoners father was in the Navy from the age of 12 (died during service, served as a boatswain on HMS Camilla), the prisoner entered into HM service at the age of 10 years, the prisoner has a brother serving in HM Navy, cannot read or write English or understand any other foreign language, he told another prisoner (William Bradley) of his plan to escape the French which has been confirmed and the prisoner wished to return to England as the prisoner has plans to marry Elizabeth Collins in Plymouth.
additional_info: There are letters from John Wiltshire and Mr Poynden of the Bridewell Hospital.

crime: publishing blasphemous libel, especially the 'Theological works of Thomas Paine' and Palmers 'Principles of nature'
grounds_clemency: nine people were tried of similar charges and all had different punishments; Ryley Perry has a sick wife to support and 2 infant children
additional_info: William Campion, Thomas Ryley Perry and John Clark, convicted at the Old Bailey in June and July 1824 for publishing blasphemous libel, especially the 'Theological works of Thomas Paine' and Palmers 'Principles of nature'. Also included is a letter from William Campion in which he states that William Haley who received the same sentence was set free because he changed his opinions and Campion accuses him of hypocrisy. Campion also states that it was made clear in his trial that the prosecution was not 'against individuals for holding such opinions, but for publishing them' and as Haley had also published the material he should not have been set free. nine people were tried of similar charges and all had different punishments; Ryley Perry has a sick wife to support and 2 infant children None recorded for Campion or Clark. Three years imprisonment in Newgate for Ryley Perry. None given for Campion or Clerk. No mercy for Thomas Ryley Perry. Folios 134-145.

additional_info: The notes were within a desk and the prisoner was seen to have extracted them with wires, by a concealed person who was put on watch due to money being missed from the desk. There is a letter from George Stone, Rector of Hopton, who had been miss-informed and that the notes were in fact left with little security, and so the prisoner was subject to 'Temptation,' and a further letter from Stone, on the same subject addressed to Mr Rey. There is a letter from A. Nicholson to William Green informing him that the prosecutor has consented to sign the petition. Nicholson has solicited help from the Reverend Mr Stone, Mr Rey and Lord Euston and includes a list of the signatories to the petition. There is a letter from Lord Euston, enclosing a letter from Mr Stone, which 'makes any addition of mine superfluous.' grounds_clemency: nine people were tried of similar charges and all had different punishments; Ryley Perry has a sick wife to support and 2 infant children
additional_info: William Campion, Thomas Ryley Perry and John Clark, convicted at the Old Bailey in June and July 1824 for publishing blasphemous libel, especially the 'Theological works of Thomas Paine' and Palmers 'Principles of nature'. Also included is a letter from William Campion in which he states that William Haley who received the same sentence was set free because he changed his opinions and Campion accuses him of hypocrisy. Campion also states that it was made clear in his trial that the prosecution was not 'against individuals for holding such opinions, but for publishing them' and as Haley had also published the material he should not have been set free. nine people were tried of similar charges and all had different punishments; Ryley Perry has a sick wife to support and 2 infant children None recorded for Campion or Clark. Three years imprisonment in Newgate for Ryley Perry. None given for Campion or Clerk. No mercy for Thomas Ryley Perry. Folios 134-145.

crime: theft/return from transportation
additional_info: The prisoner, now 24 years old, had initially been convicted by James Eyre for stealing some coals in May 1780, and now was convicted of returning before his original sentence had been completed. He had initially agreed to transport himself overseas for 7 years. After he was discharged from Newgate he went to Ostend took ship to South Carolina, went to Antigua and then back to England. He then took work on another ship the British Queen to Greenland and back again, and then on the Emmett to the South Seas. On the way home he was wrecked off Ireland and was one of 5 rescued by Captain Jennings of the Lion and brought back to Gravesend in Kent. Then he went on the Mary under Captain Bell to Greenland and back again to London where he heard that his father had died and his mother was in great distress. He went home to give her the money he had earned where he was seen by George Foreister, who found him in a closet at his mothers house. He had had a job on a ship to Guinea before being caught and his arrest stopped him going. His word was supported by Robert Stratton Lighterman who said that the ship was the brig Favourite under Captain Swann and would have been off the African Coast for 3 years. Bell stated he would be willing to employ the prisoner again.

additional_info: 8. Keziah Shepherd [Kezia Shepherd, for stealing 4 chairs and other articles, convicted in January 1794, 7 years transportation. age (54 years) 'infirm +c'. Report of Brook Watson, Mayor of London on the 'Return of Women Convicts in Newgate, where ages or infirmities will not admit of their being Transported' Signed by F. Ramsden, surgeon. John Kirby, Newgate Keeper writes, 'There are none of the 23 Women here mentioned that are of bad behaviour, and not one of them, except Jane Wiggins (who has been convicted once before) were ever before in Custody.' as the prisoners cannot 'bear a Voyage to Botany Bay' and 'their Crimes of a nature not deserving further Punishment in Newgate..., could they be sent to the Penitentiary House at Clarkenwell, they would there be in a much less Afflicting situation'. If this is not possible then a free pardon to be granted and the women sent to their respective parishes. The report is annotated 'let all the Women named in the Enclosed List be pardoned to be passed to their several parishes, except Mary Wilson and Jane Wiggins alias Young who are to be pardoned on Condition of being confined for the remainder of the term of their sentences in the House of Correction Cold Bath fields.' grounds_clemency: youth (age not given), innocent of the crime, good previous behaviour, will be 'Contaminated' by association of old offenders, bad character of the prosecutrix on whose evidence alone he was convicted, the prosecutrix had sworn against the prisoner to receive the 40 reward money (and had threatened to do so again against 3 young men who have now absconded), at court she appeared uncertain as to whether he was the right man, the real culprit was George Green who had already been executed.
additional_info: Evidences supplied by Ann Saunders, John Goodwin, Edward Clenson, the latter to whom the skins were destined, and the prisoner. The prisoner assaulted Sanders while his accomplice, George Green, ran off with the skins. Green was convicted in the June Sessions in 1789 for this robbery and executed.
